coredata.dc:description: Fast industrialization influences numerous sectors, including freshwater resources contaminated with toxic substances, which is responsible for the sharp increase in pollution. Metal-organic frameworks (MIL-88B) and the sono-photocatalysis method can remove these organic and inorganic pollutants. However, their fast recombination limits the photo & sono-photocatalytic performance. Therefore, Nitrogen and Sulfur co-doped carbon quantum dots (N,S-CQDs-doped) MIL-88B were designed here, to enhance the charge separation and associated catalysis, The crystal structure of N,S-CQDs was controlled by adjusting the synthesis temperature and compatibility of different degree of crystallinity was analyzed for composite formation with MIL-88B. N,S-CQDs synthesized at 140 °C showed better performance in composite, which was 82 % and 92 % higher than undoped MIL-88B for photo & sono-photocatalysis, respectively. However, in basic circumstances, the optimum N,S-CQDs@MIL-88B concentration showed 96 % degradation due to the significant impact of –OH groups in photocatalysis. Furthermore, the optimum N,S-CQDs@MIL-88B concentration was observed for 0.08 w/w%, resulting in 71 % and 87 % photo & Sono photocatalytic activity (PCA, SPCA) in 120 min. N,S-CQDs@MIL-88B also offers a 99 % Cr(VI) transition to Cr(III), demonstrating how it applies to organic and inorganic substances. The better performance composite is due to better light absorbance and charge separation, as confirmed by optical properties.

head.ce:title: Temperature dependent sono-photocatalysis via optimized N,S-codoped carbon quantum dots@MOF: MIL-88B composite for Cr(VI) reduction and dye degradation

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Applied Sciences, National Textile University, Faisalabad Pakistan
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Applied Sciences, National Textile University, Faisalabad Pakistan

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Textile Engineering, National Textile University, Faisalabad Pakistan
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Textile Engineering, National Textile University, Faisalabad Pakistan

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphical abstract showing sono-photocatalytic degradation of NS-CQDs@MIL-88B.

Fast industrialization influences numerous sectors, including freshwater resources contaminated with toxic substances, which is responsible for the sharp increase in pollution. Metal-organic frameworks (MIL-88B) and the sono-photocatalysis method can remove these organic and inorganic pollutants. However, their fast recombination limits the photo & sono-photocatalytic performance. Therefore, Nitrogen and Sulfur co-doped carbon quantum dots (N,S-CQDs-doped) MIL-88B were designed here, to enhance the charge separation and associated catalysis, The crystal structure of N,S-CQDs was controlled by adjusting the synthesis temperature and compatibility of different degree of crystallinity was analyzed for composite formation with MIL-88B. N,S-CQDs synthesized at 140 °C showed better performance in composite, which was 82 % and 92 % higher than undoped MIL-88B for photo & sono-photocatalysis, respectively. However, in basic circumstances, the optimum N,S-CQDs@MIL-88B concentration showed 96 % degradation due to the significant impact of –OH groups in photocatalysis. Furthermore, the optimum N,S-CQDs@MIL-88B concentration was observed for 0.08 w/w%, resulting in 71 % and 87 % photo & Sono photocatalytic activity (PCA, SPCA) in 120 min. N,S-CQDs@MIL-88B also offers a 99 % Cr(VI) transition to Cr(III), demonstrating how it applies to organic and inorganic substances. The better performance composite is due to better light absorbance and charge separation, as confirmed by optical properties.

ce:keyword.ce:text: Nitrogen and sulphur-doped carbon quantum dots

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Wastewater from the textile, drug, paper, leather, cosmetic, rubber, plastics, and pharmaceutical sectors comprises organic dyes [1], suppressing the light penetration and associated photosynthesis of aquatic plants. Similarly, it destroys the clean water reservoirs for drinking and daily use. Therefore, it is essential to create effective wastewater treatment procedures to eliminate contaminants from water and boost recycling [2]. VOCs are classified as the least important gases in the atmosphere and have posed major risks to both humanity and the natural world [3].

Apart from organic pollutants, heavy metal ions hexavalent chromium Cr(VI) is also released during industrial processes, a highly toxic and portable contaminant in wastewater, has posed a major risk to both human health and the environment [4]. Moreover, ecology would be negatively impacted by Cr(VI) in the distribution network and would severely harm human health. Therefore, wastewater Cr(VI) removal is the primary concern, for which low cost and sustainable photocatalytic reduction of Cr(VI) has gained popularity [5].

In contrast to these removal methods, advanced oxidation processes (AOPs) have demonstrated tremendous potential as effective substitutes for the breakdown of different drugs. Some instances of those methods include ozonation, photo-Fenton, photocatalysis, sonocatalysis, and sono-photocatalysis. The photocatalytic method has been widely applied to the process of purifying water and wastewater so far. A variety of substances can be eliminated via the photocatalytic method, such as heavy metals, organic compounds, organic acids, and bacteria. Its actual implementation is, however, constrained by a time-consuming process, large band gap energy (Eg), increased electron − hole (e − -h + ) recombination, and the efficacy loss brought on by various water impurities [6]. Therefore, to boost photocatalytic performance, ultrasonic breakdown of organic molecules can be employed. Ultrasound is the term used to describe sound waves that are 20–1,000 kHz beyond the human detection threshold. Ultrasonic waves cause cavitation bubbles to form in the liquid. Rapid bubble collapse generates intense confined circumstances, which involve high pressure and temperature. These extreme circumstances lead to the cleavage of dioxygen and water molecules, producing free radicals like O2 and OH. These species can oxidize the organic pollutants found in water. Nevertheless, the ultrasonic irradiation technique's numerous drawbacks, particularly its time-consuming reactions and high energy consumption, limit its potential for dye reduction. Moreover, ultrasonic irradiation exclusively is not remarkably effective for pollutant degradation. Although, particularly with their drawbacks, ultrasound, and UV radiation together seem like a potential method for pollutant degradation. Therefore, applying both photocatalysis and ultrasonic energy (sono-photocatalysis) offers a considerable number of oxidative substances resulting in a larger surface reaction rate, these substances additionally depend on reaction parameters and wastewater treatment is also fast [7]. A pair of processes may be involved simultaneously in the potential sono photocatalytic degradation pathway. The cavitation phenomenon causes hotspots, sonoluminescence, and oxygen to be produced when ultrasonic waves penetrate a fluid solution. Oxygen atoms and hotspots cause water molecules to dissociate, producing oxidizing species [8].

Therefore, composite porous materials consisting of natural and synthetic linkers known as metal–organic frameworks (MOFs) are used [9]. These materials are valuable for catalysis usage, molecular identification, segregation, drug delivery systems [10], and gas collection or stabilization because of their changeable structure, unsaturated metal sites, and structured pores [11]. Among different MOFs, the most important MOFs are Material Establishment Lavoisier's (MILs), which offers great interest in many applications, such as sodium-ion batteries, adsorption, and heterogeneous catalysis [12]. MIL-100(Fe) is a superb dispersant of CdS nanoparticles that prevents the separation of the charges produced by photolysis [13]. MIL-88B(Fe) is a Fe-based MOF with a malleable form and corresponding absorptions of visible light, which are necessary for photocatalysis. Therefore, MIL-88B (Fe) is emerging in a variety of applications due to their advantageous characteristics, like low toxic levels, wide resources, and inexpensive purification expenses in real-world applications [14]. Fe3 clusters are present in MIL-88B(Fe), which has a dicarboxylic acid ligand and a Fe (III) core that also improves photoreduction of Cr(VI) that exhibits visible-light responsive photocatalytic activity and promotes the transfer of electrons. However, the insufficient dissociation performance of photogenerated charges limits MIL-88B(Fe) and reduces its photocatalytic effectiveness [15]. Additionally, MIL-88B(Fe) has strong water stability, which is advantageous for different purposes. However, the combinations of electrons and holes that were produced when MOFs were illuminated would quickly reassemble, reducing the photocatalytic efficiency [16]. Therefore, to enhance the photocatalytic efficiency of MIL-88B(Fe), different composites of MIL-88B(Fe) were used like BiOBr/MIL-88B(Fe), Ag3PO4/Fe-MIL-88-NH2, NH2-MIL-88B, CQD@MIL-88B(Fe). Those heterostructure photocatalyst also exhibit Cr(VI) into Cr(III) transition.

Carbon quantum dots typically consist of approximately 10 nm-sized quasi-spherical graphitic nanoparticles [17] and are mixtures of hybridized carbon sp2 and sp3 with an abundance of surface functional groups. CQDs are frequently combined with different nanotubes or tiny substrates is a common way to ensure their easy recovery and prevent their rapid charge separation since their extremely small size makes it difficult for them to recover from treated water [18]. Contemporary research activities have concentrated on the synthesis of CQDs, encompassing essential methodologies like surface functionalization and mixing with distinct heteroatoms to improve their optical characteristics [19]. Moreover, tiny particle size, homogenous surface state, minimal cytotoxicity, strong biocompatibility, insensitivity to wavelengths of stimulation and surroundings, and outstanding electron-transfer rates are all characteristics of N, S-CQDs. CQDs are acceptable choices for enhancing the photocatalytic capabilities of TiO2 and ZnO photocatalysts due to their high electron resistivity. Regarding the use of CQDs in photocatalysts, recent advancements have been documented. Therefore, synthesized a TiO2/S, N-doped CQDs nanocomposite effectively catalyzes the breakdown of acid red 88 under the influence of visible light [20,21]. Similarly, ZnO nanoflowers integrated with N, S-doped CQDs for improved photocatalytic disintegrate of antibacterial whenever exposed to sunlight [22] The dye was fully degraded in under 30 min using the N-doped ZnO and CQD/N-ZnO photocatalysts, which demonstrated dye degradation yields of around 38 % and 99 %, as well, within 15 min of daylight irradiation [23].

Apart from high yield and excitation-independent emissions were demonstrated by sulfur and nitrogen co-doped CQDs. The doped nitrogen and sulfur were found to have the ability to alter the chemical composition of carbon dots [24]. Inserting nitrogen and sulfur-doped carbon quantum dots (N,S-CQDs) into ZnO to enhance sono-photocatalysis, therefore the composite promotes charge separation, increasing photocatalytic efficiency, and preventing charge recombination [25]. Similarly, most Fe-MOF photocatalysts tend to have issues with low visible light utilization and quick carrier recombination. Thus, CQDs doped in Fe-MOFs composite to enhance catalytic activity, and electron transfer rate and applied for the purification of Cr(VI). For instance, researchers verified that the NH2-MIL-88B(Fe) to Cr(VI) photo-reduction performance was far better in comparison to MIL-88B (Fe). Nevertheless, the photocatalytic capabilities of amine-functionalized Fe-based MOFs remain restricted due to significant electron-hole (e-h+) pair recombination. The most effective way to solve the problems with secondary pollution brought on by nanocomposites themselves is to develop composite structures heterogeneously on various surfaces. However, during the nanomaterials manufacturing process, doping N,S-CQDs improves charge separation, and low recombination of electron-hole pairs and promotes the homogenous solution. Therefore, N,S-CQDs@MIL-88B(Fe) composite has been developed that demonstrates improved photoactivity and Sono photocatalysis. Whereas iron-based Metal-Organic Framework (MIL-88B) has fast recombination and low solar energy conversion under UV–visible & ultrasonic irradiation. To solve this issue, synthesized MIL-88B with active N,S-CQDs (N,SCQDs@MIL-88B) that operate at various temperatures and degrade organic water pollutants. This optimal coupling of N,S −CQDs with MIL-88B occurred at 140 °C temperature and resulted in 92 % degradation in sono photocatalysis, this surpassed the 82 % degradation seen in photocatalysis with N,S-CQDs@MIL-88B. The reduced efficiency of N,S-CQDs@MIL-88B(Fe) was caused by modifications in the arrangement of crystals and a decrease in the loading catalyst. Furthermore, the ideal quantity of N,S-CQDs@MIL-88B was observed for 0.08 w/w%, identified to be 71 % and 87 % photo & Sono photocatalytic activity (PCA, SPCA) in 120 min, respectively. We also investigated the impact of temperature-induced morphology on photo & Sono photocatalysis. Moreover, N,S-CQDs@MIL-88B also offers a 99 % Cr(VI) transition to Cr(III), demonstrating how it is applicable to both organic and inorganic substances.

Materials required for the synthesis of MILL-88B, N,S-CQDs are ferric chloride hexahydrate (FeCl3·6H2O, 98.0 %), (CAS No. 10025-77-1), terephthalic acid, or 1,4 benzene dicarboxylic acid (H2BDC 97 %, CAS No. 100-21-0), N, N-Dimethylformamide (98.8 %, CAS No. 68-12-2), and sodium hydroxide (NaOH, CAS No. 1310-73-2) for the nanocomposite and Citric acid (C6H8O7, CAS No. 77-92-9), L-cysteine (C3H7NO2S, CAS No. 52-90-4), and ethanol (CAS No. 64-17-5) were all purchased from Sigma Aldrich respectively. Throughout the experiment, distilled water that had been prepared in the lab was utilized. All reagents were used directly without further purification.

Synthesis of N,S-CQDs using the hydrothermal route at different temperatures (140 °C, 140–200 °C, 200–140 °C, and 220 °C). Therefore, at 140 °C Initially, 0.86 g Citric acid (C6H8O7) and 0.65 g L-cysteine (C3H7NO2S) with the molar ratio of 1:1 Stirred for 30 min after adding 20 mL of double-distilled water. This homogeneous mixture of solvent was heated to 140 °C for eight hours in an autoclave with a 100 ml Teflon lining. The solution was dialyzed using pure water for two hours after the reaction spontaneously cooled, and the N,S-CQDs were obtained. Using the same procedure synthesis of N,S-CQDs at 140 °C–200 °C, the mixture was put into a Teflon-lined autoclave and heated to 140 °C for four hours, then to 200 °C for an additional four hours. Once the reaction was finished, the autoclave was permitted to cool naturally. Afterward, the solution is filtered by filter paper after that it is dried for one hour at 100 °C in the oven, then again filtered by vacuum pump to collect jelly-like particles. Furthermore, the Synthesis of N,S-CQDs at 200 °C–140 °C by using the same procedure, the homogenous mixture was transferred autoclave and heated initially for the first 4 h at 200 °C, subsequently for the next 4 h at 140 °C. After completing the reaction, allow the solution to cool naturally. The solution was then filtered by using a vacuum pump and purified using a dialysis membrane to remove any impurities followed by drying in an oven at 100 °C. The mixture was subsequently placed into the Teflon-lined autoclave and heated to 220 °C for six hours to synthesize N,S-CQDs. After the reaction was finished, a vacuum pump was used to collect the particles from the resultant suspension by sonicating and filtering them.

MIL-88B powder was prepared using the hydrothermal approach. Therefore, 40 mL of DMF solution was mixed with 0.81 g of FeCl3·6H2O and 0.83 g of H2BDC was stirred magnetically at room temperature. Afterward, 20 ml of distilled water mixed in 0.80 g NaOH solution was taken in a beaker and was gradually added to the solvent mixture. Consequently, these precursors were permitted to agitate for an hour at 800 revolutions per minute to produce an opaque solution. This homogeneous composite was moved to an autoclave with a 100 mL Teflon lining and heated to 220 °C for 12 h. Following the completion of the reaction and a natural cooling duration, a vacuum pump was used to separate the particles from the resulting dark-yellow suspension. The obtained dark-yellow suspension was cleaned with ethanol and distilled water, and dried for half an hour at 100 °C afterwards, the particles were collected. After being utilized again, the dry powders were stored under vacuum.

N,S-CQDs doped in MIL-88B were prepared by using the solvothermal technique. For preparation 20 ml distilled water was mixed with 0.68 g L-cysteine, and 0.52 g of citric acid of N,S-CQDs through various concentrations (0.04 w/w% 0.08 w/w% 0.12 w/w% 0.16 w/w%) add dropwise in the precursor solution of MIL-88B under continuous stirring 30 min. After being transferred into the 100 ml Teflon-lined autoclave, this uniform mixture of solvent was heated to 180 °C for 12 h. Afterward, a vacuum pump was used to filter the solution and drying in an oven at 100 °C to get particles.

The UV–visible (UV–vis, UV-3600) absorbance spectrometer confirms the N,S-CQDs@MIL-88B nanocomposite photo-catalytically efficient. A diffractometer with an X-ray spectrum (Bruker, D8 ADVANCE) was used to investigate the material's crystalline structures in the 2θ range of 20°–80°. Moreover, the functional groups of N, S-CQDs, MIL-88B, and N,S-CQDs@MIL-88B were determined by using Shimadzu Varian 4300 spectrophotometer, which operates within the frequency range of 600–4000 cm−1. Additionally, the surface morphology and phase composition of MIL-88B and N,S-CQDs@MIL-88B were determined using a scanning electron microscope (SEM, HITACHI SU8220).), transmission electron microscope (TEM, Zeiss-EM10C-100 KV), and energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) analysis on a Hitachi S4160. A photoluminescence (PL) spectrometer was used to assess the efficiency as well as the separation of charge behavior of the generated samples. A UV–visible absorbance spectrometer from Shimadzu, Japan, was used to analyze the absorbance spectra of the N,S-CQDs@MIL-88B to investigate the optical properties of the composite. Fig. 1 .

MIL-88B(Fe) is beneficial for catalytic applications, particularly for reducing Cr(VI) in photocatalysis. However, its catalytic performance is constrained by its lower energy conversion ability as well as charge recombination of electrons and hole pairs according to both visible and ultrasonic illumination. Therefore, combine photogenerated carriers with carbon-based molecules to boost their efficiency in segregation [26]. Previously, NS-CQDs were doped in a variety of semiconductors; nevertheless, this in situ doping results in crystal structural flaws and improves MIL-88B's charge separation and related catalysis. In the synthesis solution, N,S-CQDs serve as a nucleation site for in situ doping, which promotes the uniform formation of the composite [27]. Fig. 2 illustrates the coupling modes of N,S-CQDs with MIL-88B (N,S-CQDs@MIL-88B) at various temperatures (140 °C, 140 °C-200 °C, 200 °C-140 °C, and, 220 °C) in comparison to each other for photocatalysis. Reduced UV–Vis absorbance is correlated with higher dye breakdown. The electrode's photocatalytic ability towards organic pollutants was assessed with methylene blue (MB) dye as an indicator contaminant, therefore distributing a specified quantity of each photocatalyst into a certain 100 mL of a 14 ppm MB solution continuously stirred at 30 °C for 30 min in darkness [28]. The photodegradation process began thereafter by moving the solution into a reactor that was exposed to UV light or sunlight. Using UV–Vis's spectrophotometer, the mixture materials were examined every 20 min to evaluate the MB degradation. This approach was used for 120 min [29]. Photocatalytic effectiveness of N,S-CQDs@MIL-88B(Fe), and MIL-88B(Fe) was evaluated by determining the percentage change in absorbance of MB dye solution's UV vis spectra at 665 nm. Fig. 2(b) displays the photocatalytic degradation of 66 % for the MIL-88B, which increases linearly with the doping of N, and SCQDs. Therefore, when N,S-CQDs doped in MIL-88B (N,S-CQDs@MIL-88B) composites that were synthesized with different temperatures such as T140°C, T140°C −200 °C, T200°C −140 °C (high to low temperature), and T220°C showed photocatalytic degradation that is 82 %, 61.7 %, 56.6 %, and 42.4 % after 120 min, respectively as shown in Fig. 2(c-f) [30].

Amongst all coupling modes, the PCA of N,S-CQDs@MIL-88B composite that synthesis at T140°C has efficient photocatalytic activity and reduces recombination rate. By using this optimal composite doping of N,S-CQDs with different concentrations such as 0.04 %, 0.08 %, 0.12, and 0.16 % of N,S-CQDs@MIL-88B composite, photocatalytic degradation was shown at 51 %, 71 %, 42 %, and 36 %, respectively. The dye concentration drops over time for these composites are seen in Fig. 3 (b–e), and the highest decrease with the optimum concentration of N,S-CQDs@MIL-88B was observed for 0.08 w/w%, resulting in 71 % showing the largest decrease, it shows that as time increases, C/C0 lowers and, conversely, as time increases, the percentage rate of degradation also increases and improves charge separation [31]. PCA is regarded as an exceptionally promising strategy for reducing MB dye due to its high efficacy and atmospheric operating conditions. However, this approach is not suited for large-scale usage due to sluggish degradation reaction time, large band gap energy (Eg), and enhanced electron-hole (e−h+) recombination. Therefore, to boost photocatalytic performance, ultrasonic breakdown of organic molecules can be employed.

Sono-photodegradation tests were conducted simultaneously with visual and ultrasound irradiation. To assess the impact of those degradation tests on the sono-photocatalytic MB degradation, 300 µL of suspension was taken frequently, centrifugation for five minutes at 5000 rpm, and 100 µL of the precipitate was examined using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer. MB deterioration is caused by multiple configurations of sono-photocatalytic and photocatalytic activity to evaluate the degradation abilities of these various methods. The most effective catalyst, in the sono degradation of MB was subsequently examined in two additional configurations (photo and sono-photocatalysis). The US treatment clears the photocatalyst surface, preventing the buildup of impurities or chemical intermediates from the dye degradation. Additionally, ultrasonic lessened the agglomeration of catalyst microparticles in solution because of the liquid medium's turbulence, which enhances mass transfer [32].

By applying both photocatalysis and ultrasonic energy (sono-photocatalysis) offers an enormous number of oxidative substances resulting in a larger surface reaction rate, these substances additionally depend on reaction parameters and wastewater treatment is also fast. Reaction time is usually a major factor in adsorption optimization and equilibrium, particularly when treating antibiotics from wastewater and optimizing financial gains. This study examined the effects of contact times ranging from 0 to 120 min. UV–Vis spectroscopy was used to assess each sample, which was taken at different regular intervals (0, 20, 40, 60, 80, 100, and 120 min). Fig. 4 illustrates the data, which shows that the adsorption processes of MIL-88B and NS CQDs@MIL-88B with different temperatures (ST140 °C, ST140 °C −200 °C, ST200 °C-140 °C, ST220 °C) showed 72 %, 92 %, 66, 59 %, and 48 % dye degradation after 120 min, respectively, and went through two stages: an initial phase within the one hour and followed by a second stage that has equilibrium points. Therefore, adsorption capabilities often rise as contact duration increases if there are more vacant sites [33]. Amongst all coupling modes, the SPCA of N,S-CQDs@MIL-88B composite that synthesis at ST140°C has efficient sono-photocatalytic activity, reduces recombination of electron-hole pairs and improves visible light absorption.

To determine the optimal concentration of the N,S-CQDs@MIL-88B composite, the optimized N,S −CQD dosage was subsequently adjusted. Using optimal composite that synthesis at ST140°C, adjusted the N,S-CQDs concentration, which had a significant impact on evaluating SPCA [34]. For instance, the various weight percent of N,S-CQDs (0.04, 0.08, 0.12, and 0.16 w/w%) for MIL-88B precursors were studied for SPCA. It was observed that sono photocatalytic degradation of MB was shown at 67 %, 87 %, 48 %, and 60 % for 0.04, 0.08, 0.12, and 0.16 w/w% N,S-CQDs@MIL-88B respectively within 120 min. The dye concentration drops over time for these composites as seen in Fig. 5 (b-e). Consequently, we regard it as the ideal sample for catalytic degradation based on 87 % SPCA of MB via 0.08 wt% N,S-CQDs@MIL-88B composite [35]. The optimum concentration of N, S-CQDs@MIL-88B was observed for 0.08 w/w%, showing 87 % higher SPCA than MIL-88B.

Fig. 5(c) shows maximum degradation at 120 min of UV light irradiation and sonication with N,S-CQDs@MIL-88B(Fe) composite as Sono photocatalysts, approximately 87 % [36]. Moreover, for comparison, the optimum concentration (0.08 w/w%) of composite indicated that the SPCA of MB dye is 16 % greater than PCA due to the large surface area and oxidative species. Plotting (C/Co) versus irradiation time (Fig. 5a) again illustrates the degradation kinetics. Charge separation gives the Sono photocatalytic process its increased efficiency [37]. The principal method by which water contaminants are destroyed in photocatalysis and Sono photocatalysis is by the production of the OH radical. The breakdown rate is further accelerated by Sonolysis by increasing the catalytic activity in semiconductors. This might happen through a reduction in photocatalyst size and the disaggregation of particles, which increases surface area and hence the catalytic effectiveness [38].

The electrostatic interactions among the MB dye and the surface of the Sono photocatalyst in a water-based solution are influenced by the pH, which also impacts the distribution of charges on the photocatalyst. Identifying the pH range at which the high dye breakdown efficiency occurs was consequently essential. Regarding this, Fig. 6 shows the results of the SPCA tests conducted on the optimized composite and MIL-88B in both acidic and basic environments [39]. Fig. 6 displays the findings of this investigation, which range from 3 to 12, and demonstrates how the pH of the starting solution impacts dye adsorption on the N,S-CQDs@MIL-88B surface. Within basic circumstances, the composite's percentage of SPCA was slightly increased to 96 %, although in acidic circumstances it decreased and degraded to only 46 %. The breakdown of dyes requires hydroxyl ions, which are essential radicals that boost photolysis in the basic media [40]. However, when the pH level is acidic, the hydroxyl and carboxyl groups on the MIL-88B's outer layer become protonated and prevent the colors from being limited. The highest removal was seen at a pH of 12, for optimal composite N,S-CQDs@MIL-88B and successive simultaneous removal becomes less effective as pH rises [41]. As the pH of the solution rise, the proportion of MB degradation gradually increased. The largest amount of MB (methylene blue) degradation was seen at pH 12, and thereafter there was an equilibrium or decline in the degradation process. According to the influence of pH, the existence of H+ ions in acidic media and OH– ions in bases changes the photocatalytic process. Consequently, H+ ions have a greater impact on photocatalytic activity than OH– ions. Based on the findings, pH 12 was maintained for future research on dye degradation since it provided the best dye degradation [41]. Both acidic and basic pH values showed a reduction in the degradation of the MB dye. At pH 12, the Sono photocatalytic method with MIL-88B and N,S-CQDs@MIL-88B photocatalyst showed the greatest efficiency in the breakdown of MB. Less MB degradation may be caused by N,S-CQD dissolution at pH levels lower than 7. The low degradation seen at higher values of pH (9 and 12) could be attributed to the harvesting of the •OH radical at these pH levels, which reduces the chance that dye molecules would react [42]. Optimal composite 0.08 %N,S-CQDs@MIL-88B for pH12, pH9 and pH3 that show 96 %, 85 %,46 % degradation efficiency, respectively. Moreover, MIL-88B(Fe) shows 77 % and 56 % degradation efficiency in Sono photocatalysis at pH 12 & 3. The composite performed better than the MIL-88B, as was to be expected. The order of the reaction rates was N,S-CQDs@MIL-88B(pH12) > N,S-CQDs@MIL-88B(pH9) > MI-88B(pH12) > MIL-88B(pH3) > N,S-CQDs@MIL-88B(pH3) [43].

Recently, the SPCA evaluated the Acid Black 1 dye solution. Consequently, the eliminated solution demonstrated a decrease in the absorbance band at 625 nm and suggested the quick removal of the MB dye chromophore composition [44]. Based on its acidic nature, which makes it less compatible with holes and has a maximum positive charge on its surface, the data indicate that acid black I degrades less quickly than MB. As a result, the composite breaks down MB (methylene blue) instead of acid black I. Nevertheless, considering the quick massive transfer and full formation of the active sites, Cr(VI)'s lower starting concentration may be lowered quickly [45]. The photoreduction was slowed down by the limited photocatalyst adsorption sites, which left a lot of Cr(VI) in the aqueous phases and prevented the reduction reaction, as well as by the deposition of generated Cr (OH)3 on the photocatalyst surface. Fig. 7 (c) shows 99 % Cr(VI) reduction using (N,S-CQDs@MIL-88B 0.08 %) optimized sample apart from when MIL-88B(Fe) was added in chromium (VI) the Fig. 7(d) shows that only 46 % Cr(VI) was reduced [5]. Therefore, the result revealed that N,S-CQDs doped MIL-88B show better performance than undoped. According to the test outcomes, the synthesized N,S-CQDs@MIL-88B show better photocatalytic efficiency for Cr and MB due to their selective dye degradation. Positively charged compounds Cr and MB are thought to be selectively photodegraded by the surface charge of N,S-CQDs@MIL-88B [46].

To assess the crystal structure of MIL-88B and related composite structures, XRD was used. As shown in Fig. 8 (a), several peaks appeared at 23°, 28.5°, 33.2°, 35.6°, and 55° for the MIL-88B(Fe), which correspond to the (001), (111), (121), (013) and (211), planes respectively as confirmed from JCPDs case no (1341134–01-7) [47]. MIL-88B(Fe)'s distinctive peak patterns are located at 30.5° and 33°. Nevertheless, N,S-CQDs@MIL-88B(Fe) likewise exhibits the two sharp peaks at 32.8 and 35, suggesting that MIL-88B(Fe)'s crystal structure remained unchanged. In comparison to MIL-88B(Fe), the XRD arrangement of N,S-CQDs@MIL-88B(Fe) exhibits a decrease in peak intensity, indicating a loss of lattice regularity [48]. When N,S-CQDs were added in different quantities, the crystallinity of this substance was reduced and the MIL-88B(Fe) crystal structure could be recognized. N,S-CQDs diffraction peaks are not readily visible in the composite, which may be a result of the extremely dispersed nature of the N,S-CQD particles. The number of N,S-CQDs increases, shifting the diffraction peak from 28.5° to 33.2°. This is consistent with recent results showing that the diffracted peaks of some Fe-based MOFs shift and disappear as additional particles are added. Additionally, when the quantity of N,S-CQDs increased, the strength of the diffraction peak at 35.6° first increased and subsequently decreased [49]. However, crystalline size is measure using Scherrer Equation, L = Kλ/β Cosθ. The crystal dimensions of MIL-88B(Fe) and N,S-CQDs@MIL-88B(Fe) are 6.9 nm and 5.9 nm, respectively, and the peak of the N,S-CQDs indicates an additional crystalline structure.

FT-IR demonstrated that the N,S-CQDs@MIL-88B(Fe) composite comprised functional groups. As observed in Fig. 8(b) of MIL-88B(Fe), it was discovered that the absorption peaks at 1632, 1540, and 1402 cm−1 were brought on by the group stretching, symmetric, and asymmetric vibrations of C-O in carboxyl groups. C–H linking was used to link the two different bands, which were positioned at 749 and 552 cm−1. Fe-O intrinsic lattice vibrations and benzene ring vibrations in iron-oxide clusters, correspondingly, confirm the presence of Fe-oxo clusters positioned between Fe3 + and the dicarboxylate linker of H2BDC [13] The O–H and C-O stretching vibrations of L-cysteine in the N,S-CQDs are characterized by bands that lie at 3450 and 1037 cm−1, accordingly, outside of the N,S-CQD spectra. However, stretching vibrations from L-cysteine that are partially C-S and partially C-N were identified as the source of the peaks at about 1205 cm−1. Additionally, the bands at 2925 and 2964 cm−1 suggested the presence of alkyl chain groups in the N,S-CQDs, whereas the carbonyl chain groups' C=O was associated with the peak at 1700 cm−1 [25] Afterwards by doping of N,S-CQDs on MIL-88B the result shows that the catalyst may be decreased and that the peak of the S-H stretching vibrates N,S-CQDs@MIL-88B near 2525 cm−1 disappears, and C=O and C=N vibrations that compress were responsible for transformation of the carboxyl groups in citric acid's two distinct chemical environments (1749 and 1705 cm−1) into a single high-intensity peak (1735 cm−1) under hydrothermal circumstances [50]. These groups accelerate the adsorption process by producing active sites, which raises the electrode's SPCA.

Fig. 9 displays MIL-88B and N,S-CQD-doped MIL-88B SEM images at various resolutions. Depending on the synthesis circumstances, Image (a-b) is MIL-88B(Fe) crystals that can take on a variety of shapes, including hexagonal cylinders (which resemble rod-like crystals) and hexagonal bipyramids (which resemble diamond-like crystals). However, Image (c–h) are SEM images of N,S-CQDs@MIL-88B composite with different concentrations of N,S-CQDs shows that there is no change in the core of MIL-88B but their edges. Although N,S-CQDs are small, SEM detection of them is challenging. The results of XRD were validated by scanning electron microscopy (SEM), which showed that the predominant crystal form of MIL-88B(Fe) was hexagonal bipyramidal-shaped (diamond-like) crystals.

The N,S-CQDs@MIL-88B sample's synthesis was further verified by analyzing the EDX spectra (Fig. 9(i)). The findings verified that Fe (63 %), Na (3 %), O (19 %), C (9 %), and S (3 %), made up the N,S-CQDs@MIL-88B nanocomposite. The structural mapping of the N,S-CQDs@MIL-88B nanoparticles showed how the N,S-CQDs were distributed uniformly across the outermost layer of the particles [21]. Therefore, it made sense to detect a substantial number of these elements Fe, O, C, Na, S, and Cl in the mapping [51].

The chemical structure and morphology of N,S-CQDs were investigated by several characterization methods, the uniformly distributed N,S-CQDs are shown in Fig. 10 (j-k). The transmission electron microscope is a highly effective device in material science. The transmission electron microscope (TEM) image of the sample shows that these N,S co-doped carbon dots are separated from each other and have a homogeneous dispersion without evident aggregation and a narrow size distribution. To prepare the samples for detection under TEM, approximately 4 µL of sample suspension was dropped on the TEM carbon grid. The Gaussian fitting curve is used to estimate average particle size, the average particle size is (3.5 ± 0.1 nm).

The photoactivity of photocatalysts is significantly influenced by their capacity to absorb light. N,S-CQDs@MIL-88B's surface states-dependent Ultraviolet and visible light absorption was verified by an investigation of their optical characteristics. MIL-88B(Fe) may absorb light resulting from n-π* excitations in the UV spectrum, as shown by the UV–vis spectra in Fig. 11 (a). The light has a wavelength between 280 and 320 nm. The absorption curve of N,S- CQDs@MIL-88B(Fe) (at different concentrations) was more pronounced in the visible light spectrum than MIL-88B (Fe). However, the light absorption edge of MIL-88B(Fe) and N,S- CQDs@MIL-88B composite is the same, located at around 695 nm, showing its ability to absorb light [52]. Moreover, when the number of N,S-CQDs increased, the absorption edge of the N,S-CQDs@MIL-88B composite was changed. The N,S-CQD's maximal excitation and emission wavelengths were approximately 280 nm and 450 nm, respectively. The catalyst's ability to absorb intense light accelerated the process of photocatalysis. Equation, which was used to obtain the bandgap energies of MIL-88B(Fe) and N,S-CQDs@MIL-88B(Fe) from UV–vis data, can be represented as
(2) (αhν)n = A (hv − Eg)
where A is the proportionality constant, hv is the incident photon energy, α is the absorptivity, Eg is the band gap energy, and n = ½ and 2 for the indirect and direct band gaps, respectively. Although MIL-88B has a straight band gap, let us presume that n = 2. Data from the absorbance range of 249–550 nm were considered. The bandgap energy of MIL-88B(Fe) is approximately 2.13 eV, as can be shown in Fig. 11(a). Due to the small depositing amount of N,S-CQDs (0.04 w/w %), only a slight increase of absorption band is observed over the sample of N,S-CQDs@MIL-88B(Fe) in the range of 250–800 nm and have bandgap energy is approximately 2.0 eV (Fig. 11(b)). Similarly, the bandgap energies of N,S-CQDs@MIL-88B(Fe) composite exhibit 2.46 eV, 2.11 eV, and 1.75 eV, respectively with concentrations 0.08 w/w %, 0.12 w/w %, and 0.16 w/w % (Fig. 11(c-e)). Therefore, findings demonstrate that inserting N,S-CQDs to MIL-88B(Fe) efficiently extended the spectrum of light absorption and that doing so narrowed the band gap of N,S-CQDs@MIL-88B(Fe). In general, photogenerated carriers can be produced more easily with a narrow band gap [53].

The recombination rate of N,S-CQDs@MIL-88B and the transfer behavior of the photogenerated electrons and holes in a semiconductor can be studied with the use of the photoluminescence (PL) emission spectra. As seen in Fig. 11(f), the separation efficiency of the photogenerated charge carriers in the produced nanocomposites was investigated in this context using the PL spectrum. The interaction radiation was responsible for the emission peaks at approximately 694 and 710 nm. The photocatalytic activity is enhanced by a slower rate of recombination of the photogenerated charge carriers, which is indicated by lower PL emission intensity [16]. This is because slower charge transfer and electron capture at defect sites lead to quicker charge transfer. Thus by doping N, S-CQDs in MIL-88B(Fe) composite shows lower emission as compare to MIL-88B(Fe), as observed in Fig. 11(f), this indicates that the N,S-CQDs prevented the samples' photogenerated electron-hole pairs from combining. For photocatalysis, a drop in the charge recombination rate is beneficial. The literature states that the conduction band (CB) of pure MIL-88B is approximately 2.32 eV and that the LUMO level of the N,S-CQDs is in the region of 4.0–5.0 eV. Therefore, moving electrons from the CB of MIL-88B to the VB of N,S-CQDs is thermodynamically preferable. This suggests that in visible light, N,S-CQDs can boost photodegradation efficiency and decrease charge carrier recombination rate [18].

This highest degradation activity of sonophotocatalysis in comparison with photocatalysis, is attributed to ultrasonic waves. Ultrasonic waves help to produce cavitation energy followed by its transformation into small bubbles. These bubbles readily produce higher particular localized temperatures and pressure, which gear up the water molecules and yield additional hydroxyl radical –OH•, respectively. Along with superoxide radical (O2 •–), the further hydroxyl free radical (OH•) formation helps in the astonishing enhancement of the photodegradation ability of sonophotocatalysis. The Sono-photocatalytic degradation mechanism of methylene blue (MB) dye using N,S-CQDs is also shown in the graphical abstract. When N, S-CQDs@MIL-88B composites are irradiated by visible light (sunlight) and ultrasonic radiation combined (sono-photocatalysis), more reactive radicals (•OH and O• 2) are produced; these radicals remove MB dye to foam several intermediate products. Moreover, N,S-CQDs absorb lighter and reduce recombination rates by adjusting bandgap energy under sunlight and ultrasonic waves. Therefore, when exposed to sunlight and ultrasonic waves, the MIL-88B(Fe) and N,S-CQDs cooperate to form charge carriers. Thus, the doping of N,S-CQDs in MIL-88B(Fe) absorbs more light, which produces greater electron and hole pairs. That also increases the generation of hydroxyl radicals and causes the liquid to become more turbulent because of the formation and dissolution of microbubbles. The ideal energy conduction or valence bands have been used to build improved electron-hole transport channels. Consequently, it is possible to obtain the very practicable migration and separation of holes and electrons during the photocatalytic destruction of MB dye.

In this study, a structural design of N,S-CQDs@MIL-88B nanocomposite was produced using the hydrothermal technique successfully. Therefore, N,S-CQDs@MIL-88B that operate at various temperatures and compatibility of different degree of crystallinity was analyzed for composite formation with MIL-88B and degrade organic water pollutants. Accordingly results indicate that the enhanced photocatalytic & Sono photocatalytic activity of the composite is caused by the creation of a heterojunction structure and the corresponding appropriate charge separation. N,S-CQDs synthesized at 140 °C showed better performance in composite, which was 82 % and 92 % for photocatalysis and sono-photocatalysis, respectively. Similarly, the optimum concentration 0.08 w/w% of N,S-CQDs@MIL-88B demonstrated the photocatalytic and Sono photocatalytic degradation that is 71 % and 87 % of the entire, respectively. In basic circumstances, this efficiency increased to 96 %, demonstrating the significant impact of –OH groups in photocatalysis. Furthermore, N,S-CQDs@MIL-88B offers 99 % Cr(VI) reduction demonstrating that it is also appropriate for inorganic substances. The better performance of the composite is due to better light absorbance and charge separation, as confirmed by optical properties.
